title: Dog Rear Leg Tremors While Standing: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about back leg tremors in dogs that occur only while standing. Understand why it's usually low urgency and how a video helps your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rear leg tremors while standing are typically non-emergencies linked to aging or neurological conditions; recording a video of the episodes at home is the best way to help your vet reach an accurate diagnosis.

What causes rear leg tremors in dogs when they are standing still?

Rear leg tremors that occur specifically when a dog is standing still are often associated with conditions like orthostatic tremors or muscle weakness. These are involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that typically disappear as soon as the dog starts walking or lies down. In older dogs, this can also be a sign of aging or early-stage degenerative issues, while in some breeds, it may be a specific neurological condition.

Are rear leg tremors a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: Low.
  • If your dog is otherwise acting normally—meaning they have a good appetite, are able to walk without pain, and are not showing signs of distress—this is not an emergency.
  • Monitor the frequency of the shaking.
  • Schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian for a neurological and physical assessment.

How can taking a video of my dog's tremors help with a veterinary diagnosis?

  • Capture a video at home because these tremors often stop the moment your dog moves or enters a clinic environment.
  • Provide a clear view of the exact rhythm and location of the tremors for your veterinarian.
  • Help your vet differentiate between simple muscle fatigue, neurological issues, or pain-related shaking without needing to witness the event in person.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Intermittent pelvic limb tremors while standing, characterized by flexion of the hip and stifle, can be seen in dogs with specific neuromuscular diseases. This may progress to alternating flexion and extension of both pelvic limbs, giving a "dance-like" appearance, with a preference to sit rather than stand. Over time, mild paraparesis, decreased proprioception, and gastrocnemius muscle atrophy may develop. Another cause to consider is sensory ganglioneuritis, where ataxia of all limbs occurs without paresis, along with dysphagia and possible hyperesthesia, decreased proprioception, and diminished pain perception. In young animals, whole body tremors involving the limbs that lessen during rest may indicate a demyelinating disorder.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1237)

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to leg tremors?
faqs.answer: Yes, giant breeds like Great Danes and Mastiffs are more commonly diagnosed with orthostatic tremors, though muscle weakness can affect any senior dog.

faqs.question: Does my dog need immediate medication?
faqs.answer: Not usually. Since the urgency is low, your vet will first want to perform blood work or a physical exam to determine the underlying cause before prescribing any treatment.

faqs.question: Is this the same as a seizure?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike seizures, these tremors are localized to the legs, and the dog remains fully conscious and responsive throughout the episode.
